coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of blue-emitting carbon dots@mesoporous silica composite spheres
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Ziying
Zhu, Zhenpeng
Zhang, Xinguo
Chen, Yibo
coredata.dc:description: This paper reported a facile and effective approach towards high-efficient composite luminophores by embedding blue-emitting N-doped carbon dots into spherical SiO2 matrix (CDs@SiO2). Mesoporous silica microspheres (r-CDs@MSN) with strong luminescence were synthesized by removing CTAB templates in CDs@SiO2 using reflux with acetone. The r-CDs@MSN possess a spherical morphology with smooth surface and a diameter of 130 nm, while it exhibits an excitation-independent blue emission peak at 440 nm with an internal quantum yield of 21.5%. BET result shows that the corresponding surface area and adsorption total pore volume are 156.27 m2/g and 0.682 cm3/g, which is suitable for the drugs loading and release. The results indicate that r-CDs@MSN might act as a potential fluorescent drug carrier.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Luminescence
Mesoporous silica
Composite material

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence lifetime and SBET of r-CDs@MSN

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of three sample: (a) SiO2 and (b) CDs@SiO2 and (c) r-CDs@MSN and EDS spectrum for chemical composition of the as prepared r-CDs@MSN powder sample.
IR spectra of the samples: (a) SiO2 and (b)CDs@SiO2 and (c) r-CDs@MSN.
SEM imaging of different sample: (a) SiO2 and (b) CDs@SiO2 and (c) r-CDs@MSN.
TEM photos of r-CDs@MSN. It can more clearly observe its morphology and size.
The excitation and emission spectra of r-CDs@MSN (Fig. 5a) and the sample under UV light illumination (Fig. 5b).
Different excited wavelength on the fluorescence intensity of r-CDs@MSN (Fig. 6a) and different exposed time of UV radiation (Fig. 6b).
Fluorescence decay fitted curves of CDs@SiO2 (Fig. 6a) and r-CDs@MSN (Fig. 6b) with excitation wavelength at 360 nm.
Isothermal adsorption and desorption curves of r-CDs@MSN and attaching pore size analysis.
Schematic explains the preparation of porous r-CDs@ MSN.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of blue-emitting carbon dots@mesoporous silica composite spheres

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou 510515, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou Key Laboratory for Environmentally Functional Materials and Technology, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Guangzhou Key Laboratory for Environmentally Functional Materials and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reported a facile and effective approach towards high-efficient composite luminophores by embedding blue-emitting N-doped carbon dots into spherical SiO2 matrix (CDs@SiO2). Mesoporous silica microspheres (r-CDs@MSN) with strong luminescence were synthesized by removing CTAB templates in CDs@SiO2 using reflux with acetone. The r-CDs@MSN possess a spherical morphology with smooth surface and a diameter of 130 nm, while it exhibits an excitation-independent blue emission peak at 440 nm with an internal quantum yield of 21.5%. BET result shows that the corresponding surface area and adsorption total pore volume are 156.27 m2/g and 0.682 cm3/g, which is suitable for the drugs loading and release. The results indicate that r-CDs@MSN might act as a potential fluorescent drug carrier.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging fluorescent material, the carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention due to their remarkable advantages of low toxicity, luminescence stability, non-blinking and biocompatibility [1–4]. These excellent properties make it suitable for potential applications in optoelectronic devices, cell imaging, heavy metal testing and drug release [5–8]. Currently, numerous approaches with different raw materials have been used for CDs synthesis. The reported raw materials range from synthetic chemicals to natural products, which included citric acid, glucose, phenylenediamines, bagasse and vegetables [9–13], etc, whereas the methods consist of eletrophoretic, laser ablation, electrochemical, combustion, microwave and hydrothermal [14–19]. Among these methods, hydrothermal method has gradually received favorable because it is facile, green and economical. In many cases, carbon dots can be directly used for imaging/sensing application without further modifications. For example, Jiang et al. [11] have reported the full-color emission of the CDs was applied for multicolor cellular imaging. It has been found by Zong et al. [20] that carbon dots were synthesized as fluorescence probe for Cu2+ detection. Simultaneous, Qu et al. [21] have prepared the carbon dots by hydrothermal as an effective fluorescence sensing platform. Besides, carbon dots have been indirectly used by Sahu et al. [22] for excellent bio-imaging agent.

In recent years, the composites of other materials and carbon dots have aroused general interest. A wide range of materials have been exploited to integrate with CDs such as calcium carbonate microparticle, sodium chloride, rare earth materials, silica [23–26], etc. Among these substances, the mesoporous silica possessing many appealing merits such as a variety of structural morphology, controlled micro-size, easily functionalized surface and ultra high surface area [27], have been widely investigated in various applications (catalysis, optics, adsorption, etc.). They are also nontoxic and biocompatible and their composites have pronounced osteogenic properties, which is extremely suitable as drug carrier. But the procedure of drug delivery system cannot be tracked in vivo utilizing the pure mesoporous silica because of the lack of detection signals. Taking into account the high signal to noise ratio (SNR) and low cytotoxicity, carbon dots combine with mesoporous silica become a hotspot. CDs can be blinker to the entrance of MSNs to control monitor the drug release with luminescent signal variation.

In this work, carbon dots@mesoporous silica composite spheres were synthesized via a facile and effective method. Firstly, the blue-emitting carbon dot solution was synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid as carbon resource. As shown in the schematic illustration (Scheme 1 ), using CTAB as template, the blue emitting CDs were then directly combined with TEOS for further synthesis of CDs@SiO2 composites, which exhibits strong blue luminescence under UV irradiation. The subsequent treatment with refluxing acetone could remove CTAB templates and the as-obtained sample possesses the mesoporous structure. The structure and fluorescence properties of as-prepared carbon dot@mesoporous silica composites were then explored, which may be a promising candidate for drug carrier.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, urea, ethanol absolute, diethanolamine, Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium Bromide (CTAB), silicic acid tetracthyl ester (TEOS). All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

The bCDs were prepared through the hydrothermal method [28]. Firstly, 0.42 g citric acid was dissolved with 10 ml deionized water, into which 0.536 ml ethylenediamine was injected at room temperature. Then, the solution was transferred into stainless steel autoclave and was heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The crude product was purified via dialysis bag (Mw = 1000) for 24 h.

The synthesis of CDs@SiO2 particles were as follows. 7 ml ethanol, 50 μL diethanolamine and 25 ml blue CDs were put into the beaker containing 0.2 g CTAB, forming the mixed solution. Then, the mixture was stirred half hour at 60 °C. After 0.5 h, 2 ml TEOS was added in rapidly and maintained stirring 2 h. The resultant was collected by centrifuging at 8000 rpm 5 min and the precipitate was put into oven at 60 °C overnight. In order to obtain porous r-CDs@ MSN, taking partial above CDs@SiO2 into three neck flask, 100 ml acetone was added at 80 °C refluxed 17 h. Then, the final products were received after purification (8000 rpm 5 min) and drying at 60 °C for 24 h.

The FT-IR spectrum was tested in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 with PerKin EImer FT-IR spectrometer Frontier using KBr tablet. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the substance was measured with Rigaku D/max-IIB X-ray diffractometer with 2-theta ranged from 10° to 70°. The morphology of samples were observed through the field emission scanning electron microscopy (JSM-7001F). Using Transmission electron microscopy to analyze the shape and size of the products, a JEM-2100F was applied which had an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The fluorescence spectroscopy and decay curves of r-CDs@MSN were collected on FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The surface area and pore size analysis of samples were obtained on JW-BK200C static microporous analyzer operating at nitrogen atmosphere and the adsorption temperature of 77 k. Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) was collected on JSM-6700F Cold Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (JEOS, Japan) to analyze the chemical composition of the powder samples. The optical absorbance efficiency (AE) and internal quantum yield (IQE) of r-CDs@MSN was operated on HITACHI F7000 spectrometer with excitation wavelength at 360 nm. The AE and IQE were calculated according to the equation:
A E =
∫
λ
{
E
( λ )
− R
( λ )
}
d λ
∫
λ ⋅ E
( λ )
d λ
I Q E =
∫
λ ⋅ P
( λ )
d λ
∫
λ
{
E
( λ )
− R
( λ )
}
d λ
Where
E
( λ )
/
h v
,
R
( λ )
/
h v
,
P
( λ )
/
h v
are the number of photons in the spectrum of excitation, reflectance, and emission of the sample, respectively.

XRD patterns of SiO2 (a), CDs@SiO2 (b) and r-CDs@MSN (c) are shown in Fig. 1 , which revealed that the broad diffraction peak was emerged at 23°, which indicates the existence of amorphous silica. The patterns of all samples show no significant difference, which means that the phase of silica is stable in spite of combining with CDs or after refluxing. The chemical composition of the r-CDs@MSN is shown in Fig. 1. EDS on the as prepared powder samples confirmed the existence of CDs in the SiO2 matrix. As shown in Fig. 2 , the FT-IR spectrum featured several characteristic absorption banks of SiO2 (Fig. 2a), in which peak at the 1080 cm−1、462 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration and bending vibration of Si-O-Si, respectively. And those at 3435 cm−1, 1638 cm−1 can be corresponded to the stretching vibration peak of the hydroxyl group. The peaks of CTAB [29](2921 cm−1, 2851 cm−1) were disappeared after acetone reflux process (Fig. 2c), which confirmed that the successful remove of CTAB template and the formation of mesoporous silica sphere was produced.

Fluorescence properties of r-CDs@MSN can be seen from Fig. 5 , which displayed that a broad peak with strongest at 440 nm upon excited at 360 nm (Fig. 5a). The internal quantum efficiency is a significant parameter for fluorescence intensity. In order to measure the absolute quantum efficiency of r-CDs@MSN, the absorbance efficient (AE) and internal quantum efficiency (IQE) were determined applying the integrated sphere approach. The value of AE and IQE of the r-CDs@MSN under 360 nm excitation are determined to be 50 % and 21.5 %, respectively. In addition, the r-CDs@MSN sample emits bright blue light under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 5b). Moreover, it is shown that the as-prepared r-CDs@MSN exhibits excitation-independent emission behavior with excitation wavelengths from 330 nm to 390 nm (Fig. 6 a) with an interval of 10 nm. As reported by Long et al. [31], surface state and particle size distribution of the composite may be explained this behavior, implying that the sample are uniform in the size and surface. Unlike many other reported CDs [32], the r-CDs@MSN has a stable single-emitting peak without wavelength shift when different excitation wavelengths were applied. However, the fluorescence intensity varies with the excitation wavelength, and the maximum fluorescence intensity of r-CDs@MSN can be achieved when excited at 360 nm (Fig. 6a). Thus, r-CDs@MSN exhibits good photo-stability since the fluorescence intensity exhibits no significant quenching under UV irradiation up to 2 h (Fig. 6b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence lifetime and SBET of r-CDs@MSN
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence lifetime of r-CDs@MSN (Fig. 7 ) was measured to obtain the decay nature of fluorescence, which is successfully fitted with bi-exponent decay kinetics functions.
I =
I 0
+
A 1
exp
(
−
t
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
(
−
t
τ 2
)
Where I and I
0 is the luminous intensity monitored at time t and zero, A
1 and A
2 are constants, and τ
1 and τ
2 are the short and long lifetimes for exponential components. A short component (τ
1 = 6.63 ns) and a long component (τ
2 = 15.20 ns) of CDs@SiO2 is compared with r-CDs@MSN (τ
1 = 5.68 ns and τ
2 = 13.88 ns). The average decay life (τ*) could be determined by following equation:
τ ∗
=
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2

The value of τ* for CDs@SiO2 and r-CDs@MSN samples are found to be 11.44 ns and 7.883 ns, respectively. The results suggest that radiative recombination of the excitons is the most likely reason of the blue CDs with such short decay lifetimes. The different fluorescence lifetime may be due to the process of reflux, which leaded to the variation of surface state of the sample. So the radiative recombination of excitons with the holes decreases and the excitons decay through two different paths [33]. By the BET analysis, the datas of surface area, pore volume and pore size could be acquired, which can confirm the removal of CTAB template and the formation of mesoporous structure. Fig. 8 shows the isothermal adsorption and desorption curves of r-CDs@MSN sphere, which demonstrates that the surface area S BET of sample is 156.27 m2/g and adsorption total pore volume is 0.682 cm3/g, while the average pore size is 17.46 nm. Compared with the previously reported porous calcium carbonate [34] with the S BET = 40.02 m2/g and V mes = 0.23 cm3/g, the SBET of r-CDs@MSN is 4 times bigger than that of CaCO3 particles. As a result, it implied that the r-CDs@MSN with excellent advantage is more suitable as a drug delivery system.

In summary, we have demonstrated a facile and low-cost approach to prepare blue-emitting carbon dot using citric acid as the carbon precursor. The as-prepared CDs have been successfully combined with mesoporous SiO2, forming the blue-emitting carbon dot@mesoporous silica (r-CDs@MSN) composites. More importantly, CDs enable composites emitting bright blue fluorescence under an UV irradiation with an IQE of 21.5%. Additionally, the r-CDs@MSN exhibited excitation-independent emission behavior with strongest peak at 440 nm upon excited at 360 nm. The BET result shows that r-CDs@MSN has large pore volume and super-high specific surface area. The attractive fluorescence properties, mesoporous structure, and low toxicity of r-CDs@MSN make it promising candidates in the aspect of cell imaging, heavy metal testing, and drug carriers, etc.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2017A030313255), Scientific Research Starting Foundation of Southern Medical University (No. PY2016N004) and the Open Project of Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening. We thank Dr. Yibo Chen (School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou University) for SEM and quantum efficiency measurement. The corresponding author (X.G. Zhang) sincerely thanks his beloved, Miss. Lucia (Congying) Chen (Guangzhou Science Technology and Innovation Commission, China), for her help in polishing the article.
